data.title: COMMENT-Trump-Starmer warmth is positive for King's currency
data.description:
Sterling bulls have reasons to be cheerful after Thursday's White House meeting between Donald Trump and Keir Starmer, which included Trump accepting an invitation from King Charles for a future state visit.

The bonhomie between the U.S. President and UK Prime Minister suggests Britain might be spared tariffs on goods exports to the United States.

Trump said the U.S. and Britain are negotiating a bilateral trade agreement, saying such a deal could help avert U.S. tariffs.

The president's intimation came 24 hours after he threatened to impose 25% tariffs on imports from the European Union - a threat which hurt the euro.

The contrast between Trump's attitude towards tariffs on British and EU goods is a source of support for GBP/EUR, which scaled a 10-week peak of 1.2132 on Thursday (1.2161 was December's 33-month high).

Related comments:

For more click on

data.title: Primary Health Buys Irish Property for EUR22 Million
data.description:
Primary Health Properties (, PHP.JO) said Friday it acquired a health and wellbeing clinic in Cork, Ireland, for 22 million euros.

The healthcare facility investor added that the fully leased property has an unexpired lease term of 12 years with fixed rental uplifts in 2027 and 2032.

data.title: Hong Kong Shares Fall, Marks Biggest Daily Loss Since October — Market Talk
data.description: Hong Kong shares ended lower after President Trump announced an additional 10% tariff on Chinese imports, spooking investors. Trump cited China's role in the fentanyl trade as the main reason for the new tariffs, which led investors to turn to de-risking, IG market strategist Yeap Jun Rong said. The benchmark Hang Seng Index closed 3.3% lower at 22941.32, marking its largest one-day loss since October 2024. The Hang Seng Tech Index dropped 5.3%. Nearly all sectors lost ground, with BYD Electronic leading the losses, falling 10.5%. Longfor Group dropped 8.9%, Li Auto and Geely Automobile fell 8.1% and 8.0%, respectively. Among the few gainers, Galaxy Entertainment rose 1.9% and Sands China gained 0.45%.(jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

data.title: Tropicana Repurchases More Shares
data.description:
Tropicana bought back 472,000 shares for 540,466 ringgit on Thursday, according to a same-day Malaysian bourse disclosure.

The shares were repurchased by the real estate firm at between 1.140 ringgit and 1.150 ringgit apiece, the filing said.

data.title: Weir Group's Micromine Acquisition to Boost 2025 Earnings — Market Talk
data.description: Weir Group's full-year 2024 results were boosted by accelerating demand in its aftermarket business and a strong operational performance over the second half of the year, Shore Capital analysts Akhil Patel and Tom Fraine write. Weir also announced the acquisition of mining software solutions company Micromine for 657 million pounds. The business should contribute around 34 million pounds in revenue in 2025, the analysts write. It is expected to make an earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization contribution of 16.5 million pounds, they add. Shares trade up 0.35% at 22.94 pounds. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

data.title: Beijing GeoEnviron Engineering & Technology Wins Food Waste Treatment Bid
data.description:
Beijing GeoEnviron Engineering & Technology won the bid for the Baise City kitchen waste treatment and resource utilization project in Guangxi, China for a price of 273.88 yuan per ton.

The project involves building a 150-ton-per-day facility in two phases, focusing on food and cooking oil waste treatment, according to a Thursday filing with the Shanghai bourse.

The 30-year agreement is expected to produce bio-oil, bio-carbon and organic fertilizers.

Shares of the environmental management system solutions provider were down nearly 2% Friday.

data.title: Higher EU Fiscal Spending Could Offset ECB Rate Cuts for Bonds — Market Talk
data.description: Expectations of higher EU fiscal spending and balance-sheet reduction by the European Central Bank could largely offset the impact from lower interest rates on bonds, says SEB Research's Jussi Hiljanen in a note. SEB maintains a view that the 10-year German Bund yield will continue to trade in a range between 2.20% and 2.60% in the coming months, the chief strategist for euro and dollar rates says. Eurozone 10-year bond yields trade around 3 basis points lower across the board, with the 10-year Bund yield down 3 basis points at 2.383%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: International Paper names new CFO
data.description:
International Paper said on Friday that Lance Loeffler would be its chief financial officer, replacing Tim Nicholls, who will head the company's newly acquired DS Smith.

Loeffler's 25-year career includes finance and business leadership roles at UBS , Deutsche Bank and Halliburton .

He was Halliburton's CFO for four years and most recently was senior vice president of the Middle East and North Africa region. He left in December, according to his LinkedIn profile.

Nicholls, International Paper's CFO since 2018, will now be executive vice president and president of DS Smith, which the company acquired for about $7.20 billion last month.

International Paper has been streamlining its U.S. operations as higher prices hit sales volumes. Earlier this month, it said it would close four plants and lay off about 1% of its global workforce, affecting 674 employees.

The company did not say when the executives would take charge in their new roles.

data.title: What is the distribution of forecasts for the US PCE?
data.description:
Why it's important?

The ranges of estimates are important in terms of market reaction because when the actual data deviates from the expectations, it creates a surprise effect. Anotherimportant input in market's reaction is the distribution of forecasts.

In fact, although we can have a range of estimates, most forecasts might be clustered on the upper bound of the range, so even if the data comes out inside the range of estimates but on the lower bound of the range, it can still create a surprise effect.

Distribution of forecasts for PCE

PCE Y/Y

  • 2.7% (3%)
  • 2.6% (3%)
  • 2.5% (47%) - consensus
  • 2.4% (47%)

PCE M/M

  • 0.4% (18%)
  • 0.3% (74%) - consensus
  • 0.2% (8%)

Core PCE Y/Y

  • 2.8% (3%)
  • 2.7% (5%)
  • 2.6% (71%) - consensus
  • 2.5% (21%)

Core PCE M/M

  • 0.4% (4%)
  • 0.3% (80%) - consensus
  • 0.2% (16%)

The market will focus on the Core PCE readings. We can see that the expectations are skewed to the downside, so a higher than expected number wouldn't be good news.

data.title: Global Net Lease Q4 AFFO Rises, Revenue Falls
data.description:
Global Net Lease reported Q4 adjusted funds from operations late Thursday of $0.34 per diluted share, up from $0.31 a year earlier.

Analysts polled by FactSet expected $0.31.

Revenue for the quarter ended Dec. 31 was $199.1 million, compared with $206.7 million a year earlier.

Analysts surveyed by FactSet expected $194.3 million.

The company said it expects 2025 adjusted funds from operations of $0.90 to $0.96 per share. Analysts surveyed by FactSet expect $1.21.

data.title: British energy secretary to visit China in March to restart energy talks, sources say
data.description:
Key points:
  • Labour government seeks to improve UK-China relations
  • Miliband to discuss clean energy, not nuclear, with China
  • Miliband to also talk to investors in China about UK opportunities

British Energy Secretary Ed Miliband will visit China in March to restart talks on energy cooperation and meet Chinese investors, three sources with direct knowledge of the plan said, as the Labour government seeks closer China ties amid worsening U.S.- and EU-China relations.

The sources, who were not authorised to speak to media, said Miliband will visit Beijing on March 17-19, which Reuters is reporting for the first time.

British officials have said they want to recalibrate many of the previous Conservative Party-led government's positions on China, particularly around accepting Chinese job-creating investment and allowing Chinese firms to provide critical infrastructure, such as nuclear power plants.

Nuclear power will still be off the table when Miliband is expected to sit down on March 17 with his Chinese counterpart, Wang Hongzhi, to revive the UK-China Energy Dialogue, two of the sources said. Collaboration in other forms of clean and sustainable energy, as well as energy security, will be on the agenda instead.

Miliband will also pitch Britain to Chinese private investors at a roundtable meeting, said one of the sources, adding that Miliband's schedule was still being finalised.

Britain's Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office and China's foreign ministry did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

The Labour government, in power in Britain since July, has made improving ties with China one of its main foreign policy goals, after a period when relations plunged to their lowest levels in decades under successive Conservative governments.

In 2022, then-Prime Minister Rishi Sunak signed off on providing around 700 million pounds ($880 million) to buy out China General Nuclear in a nuclear power project in the south west of England after some British lawmakers expressed concerns about China's involvement in the country's nuclear industry.

Britain has refrained from following the U.S. and Europe in slapping import tariffs on Chinese electric vehicles over accusations its automakers benefit from unfair state subsidies and have overcapacity.

Chinese automakers BYD and Chery (CHERY.UL) both sell into the UK market, while XPeng plans to launch its cars in Britain later this year.

BYD also manufacturers London's iconic red double-decker buses, while a subsidiary of Geely (GEELY.UL) makes the city's famous black taxi cabs.

Miliband will be the third Labour minister to visit China since Prime Minister Keir Starmer took office, following Foreign Secretary David Lammy's trip in October and finance minister Rachel Reeves' visit in January.

Starmer is also expected to visit China later this year, sources said, in the first trip to the country by a British leader since 2018.

China's foreign minister Wang Yi earlier this month made his first official visit to Britain in a decade.

Reuters reported last week that Britain has lined up a new Mandarin-speaking diplomat to help rebuild its China ties.

China is Britain's fifth-largest trading partner and accounts for 5.2% of UK trade, with the world's second-largest economy taking in 32 billion pounds ($40 billion) worth of UK goods last year.

($1 = 0.7948 pounds)

data.title: New World Development reports $852 million net loss in H1
data.description:
Hong Kong's New World Development reported on Friday a net loss of HK$6.63 billion ($852.45 million) for the first half ended in December, hurt by impairment and fair-value losses.

The figure, which counts only continuing operations, compares with a HK$502 million net profit a year earlier and follows a record HK$11.8 billion net loss for the full 2023/2024 financial year.

($1 = 7.7776 Hong Kong dollars)

data.title: Forvia Shares Plummet After Guidance Falls Short of Expectations
data.description:
By Adria Calatayud

Forvia shares fell sharply after the French auto-parts supplier issued guidance that fell short of analysts' expectations and said it wouldn't declare a dividend to focus on reducing debt.

Shares in Forvia fell 18% in early morning trade in Europe, which would be the worst one-day percentage drop ever for the company if maintained until close.

The company said it expects 2025 sales to range from 26.3 billion to 27.5 billion euros ($27.35 billion-$28.60 billion) and an operating margin of between 5.2% and 6.0%. Forvia said its guidance takes into account U.S. tariffs already in place, but not potential additional levies.

Analysts had forecast 2025 sales of 27.76 billion euros and an operating margin of 5.8%, according to consensus estimates provided by Visible Alpha.

Moreover, the company's board decided to declare no dividend for the year to prioritize its debt-reduction efforts.

While the absence of a dividend was anticipated, the weaker-than-expected outlook will be taken negatively, analysts at Bernstein wrote in a note to clients.

For 2024, Forvia reported a 1% decline in sales to 26.97 billion euros. Operating profit fell 2.7% to 1.40 billion, with a margin that contracted to 5.2% from 5.3% a year before.

Forvia Chief Executive Patrick Koller said the company navigated a difficult environment last year, as global automotive production fell in the second half.

"The uncertainty in the European market, largely attributable to the continued slowdown of electrification, and the high level of car inventories in North America, contributed to this unfavorable second-half environment," Koller said.

Write to Adria Calatayud at adria.calatayud@wsj.com

data.title: Czech Republic Q4 GDP Revised Higher
data.description:
The Czech Republic's GDP grew by 0.7% quarter-on-quarter in the fourth quarter of 2024, above the preliminary estimates of 0.5% rise and edging up from an upwardly revised 0.6% increase in the previous three-month period, second estimates showed.

This marked the strongest expansion in Czech economy since Q1 of 2022, primarily attributed to household expenditure (1.5% vs 0.9%).

However, government spending (-0.3% vs 0.8%) slipped, as well as the gross capital formation (-0.4% vs 3.4%).

On net external demand, exports fell 1.5% (vs 2%) while imports declined 1.8% (vs 3.6%).

On a yearly basis, the economy expanded by 1.8% in the fourth quarter of 2024, higher than preliminary estimates of 1.6% gain, the fastest pace since Q3 of 2022, and compared to a 1.4% advance in the previous three-month period.

data.title: TTF Prices Up for 2nd Session
data.description:
European natural gas futures rose 2% to €46 per megawatt-hour on Friday, following a 9% gain in the previous session.

This rebound comes after a significant drop from a two-year high of €59 on February 10th.

Traders are seizing the opportunity to buy amidst concerns that a potential US-EU trade dispute could raise LNG costs, especially as the US is Europe’s main supplier.

President Trump threatened 25% tariffs on EU imports, including cars, starting April 2.

Despite the increase, gas prices remain over 20% below February's peak, driven by mild weather, potential easing of EU storage mandates, and US-led peace talks in Ukraine.

With EU gas storage levels below 40%, major European nations are considering relaxing storage rules to stabilize supply ahead of next winter.

For the week, European natural gas prices are on track for a modest increase after falling for two consecutive weeks.

data.title: RBC Lifts Beiersdorf Price Target on Consumer Business Strength
data.description:
RBC Capital Markets raised its price target for Beiersdorf to 120 euros from 117 euros following the German personal-care products manufacturer's full-year 2024 results.

The research firm maintained a sector perform rating on the stock.

Analysts highlighted the continued strength of Beiersdorf's consumer business, which they expect to grow at the upper end of the company's 4% to 6% guidance range in 2025, despite a weaker global skin care market and inventory challenges in China. RBC forecasts consumer organic revenue growth of 5.4%, driven by strong demand for Eucerin and Nivea, along with new product launches.

For 2025, analysts now expect a group revenue of 10.30 billion euros, down from a prior estimate of 10.51 billion euros, while EPS is projected at 4.20 euros, compared with 4.25 euros previously. For 2026, revenue and EPS are expected to reach 10.90 billion euros and 5.27 euros, respectively, versus earlier estimates of 11.08 billion euros and 5.08 euros.

"Our FY25 Group organic revenue growth estimate of +4.8% and EBIT margin improvement of 30bps are in line with guidance. Our EPS estimates increase to reflect the announced (Euro)500mn share buyback," RBC wrote.

data.title: French Inflation Cools to Lowest in Four Years as ECB Prepares to Cut Rates Again — Update
data.description:
By Joshua Kirby

France's inflation rate fell to its lowest level in four years, taking it further below the European Central Bank's target and priming the bank for a fresh cut to interest rates next week.

Consumer prices in the country rose 0.9% on year this month, falling sharply from 1.8% in January, according to EU-harmonized figures published Friday by France's statistics authority. That was considerably lower than the 1.5% rate economists had estimated in a poll compiled by The Wall Street Journal.

Falling energy prices made up much of the drop, but prices of services also lost pace.

The latest data shows inflation is at its lowest level since February 2021, before a series of supply shocks drove it higher across Europe and much of the globe.

It also marks the first time since September that annual inflation has fallen in France, and means the rate has remained below the ECB's 2% target for a full half-year.

For the wider 20-member eurozone, inflation is trending above that target. But signs of cooler price rises in France, alongside stability in Spain, another major eurozone economy, are likely to steel ECB rate setters' determination to lower the bank's key rate for a sixth time since last summer when the governing council meets in Frankfurt next week.

Inflation figures for Europe's largest economy, Germany, will be set out later Friday, and those for the eurozone as a whole on Monday.

A fall in regulated electricity prices is largely to thank for France's cooler inflation this month, economists Stephane Colliac and Guillaume Derrien at bank BNP Paribas wrote in a note. But the underlying trend points to feebler rises across the board, they said.

"Disinflation is becoming increasingly widespread," Colliac and Derrien said.

Cooler consumer prices in much of the eurozone have been coupled with a lack of momentum in the currency area's collective economy. The French and German economies both contracted at the end of last year, and the wider eurozone saw only anemic growth. Lower interest rates have offered some succor to European businesses, but confidence in the private sector remains below the long-term average, surveys this week showed.

That glum mood comes amid the looming threat of high tariffs on European goods exported to the U.S. and a likely retaliation in kind on American goods sold in Europe. President Trump said this week that he would enforce a 25% tariff on "cars and all the things" that Europe makes and sells to the U.S, though he didn't set out a timetable. That follows his plan outlined earlier this month to add a steep tax on European steel and aluminum imports. A trade battle could drive up inflation in Europe, some ECB policymakers warn, though others have said the main effect will be to put the brakes on the continent's economic recovery.

Write to Joshua Kirby at joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby

data.title: REG - IMI PLC - Share repurchase programme
data.description:
RNS Number : 8787Y IMI PLC 28 February 2025  

28 February 2025

IMI plc

Share repurchase programme

IMI plc (the Company) announces that, in connection with the share buyback of up to £200 million announced earlier today, it is commencing a share buyback programme (the "Programme") to purchase ordinary shares of 28 4/7 p in the capital of the Company ("Shares") with a value of up to £200 million, during the period starting on 28 February 2025 and ending no later than 30 September 2025 (the "Engagement Period"), in order to reduce the capital of the Company. Shares purchased pursuant to the Programme will be cancelled.

The Company has entered into an agreement with Numis Securities Limited ("Deutsche Numis") to manage the first £100 million tranche of the Programme (the "First Tranche"). Deutsche Numis will carry out the acquisition of Shares in the Company (on a riskless principal basis) for subsequent repurchase by the Company. Any further tranches of the Programme, which may be conducted after completion of the First Tranche, will be announced in due course.

This arrangement is in accordance with Chapter 9 of the Financial Conduct Authority's UK Listing Rule and the Company's general authority granted by shareholders at the Company's annual general meeting held on 9 May 2024, which limits purchases of Shares by the Company in the market to up to approximately 10% of the Company's issued share capital, being 26,146,669 Shares*. The Programme will also be effected within the parameters of the Market Abuse Regulation 596/2014/EU and the Commission Delegated Regulation 2016/1052/EU (in each case, as they form part of UK law by virtue of the European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018 as amended from time to time, including where relevant pursuant to the Market Abuse (Amendment) (EU Exit) Regulations 2019).

Enquiries to:

  • Louise Waldek

  • IMI

  • Tel: +44 (0)121 717 3780

*The existing authority to buy back Shares granted at the Company's 2024 Annual General Meeting will expire at the earlier of 1 July 2025 or the Company's 2025 Annual General Meeting, where the Company expects to seek renewal of that authority.

data.title: REG - Martin Currie GblPrt - Net Asset Value(s)
data.description:
RNS Number : 8789Y Martin Currie Global Portfolio Tst 28 February 2025      Martin Currie Global Portfolio Trust plc (the "Company")   Franklin Templeton Investment Trust Management Limited, as Company Secretary, announces the unaudited net asset values (on the AIC basis) of the Company as at the close of business on 27 February 2025  With debt valued at par excluding income: 364.74p per ordinary share.  With debt valued at market excluding income: 364.74p per ordinary share.  With debt valued at par including income: 364.75p per ordinary share.  With debt valued at market including income: 364.75p per ordinary share.    28 February 2025 This information is provided by RNS, the news service of the London Stock Exchange. RNS is approved by the Financial Conduct Authority to act as a Primary Information Provider in the United Kingdom. Terms and conditions relating to the use and distribution of this information may apply. For further information, please contact rns@lseg.com or visit www.rns.com.

RNS may use your IP address to confirm compliance with the terms and conditions, to analyse how you engage with the information contained in this communication, and to share such analysis on an anonymised basis with others as part of our commercial services. For further information about how RNS and the London Stock Exchange use the personal data you provide us, please see our Privacy Policy.  END  NAVPPUMPPUPAURR

data.title: Terms for switches in March to inflation-linked bond SGB IL 3116
data.description:
The Swedish National Debt Office offers switch auctions in the 3116 inflation-linked bond maturing on 1 June 2036, aiming to build up volume in the new bond. The switch auctions will be held on March 21 and 24 and the total volume offered is SEK 1 billion.

In the switch auctions, the Debt Office plans to buy back SGB IL 3114 0.125% 1 Jun 30 and SGB IL 3111 0.125% 1 Jun 32. The switch against SGB IL 3114 will be made cash neutral[1] whereas the switch against SGB IL 3111 will be made price risk neutral [2].

Switch auctions

Fri 21 MarCash neutralMon 24 MarRisk neutral
SNDO buys bond31143111
SNDO buys amount (SEK million) [3]420580
SNDO sells bond31163116
SNDO sells amount (SEK million)500500
Time for yield on buy-back bond10.4510.45
Time for auction cut-off11.0011.00
Time for auction result11.0311.03

The switch auctions were announced in the publication Central Government Borrowing – Forecast and Analysis 2024:3 in November. The purpose of the switches is to build up volume in the new bond.

The bid yield on the buy-back bond will be published at 10:45 on the day of the auction on riksgalden.se/borrowing. Bids must be placed before 11:00 through the Debt Office’s primary dealers. The results will be published at 11:03 or somewhat later.

For more information, please contact:Johan Bergström, Head of Funding, +46 (0)8 613 45 68Anna Olofsson, Head of Analysis, +46 (0)8 613 45 98

[1] The market value of the purchase and sales volume of each bond shall be equal.

[2] The market value of the purchase and sales volume multiplied by the modified duration of each respective bond shall be equal.

[3] The stated nominal volume of the bonds 3114 and 3111 is an approximation based on today's yields.
